does: This paper reveals that user-side memory in large language models exhibits asymmetric failures hidden by aggregate personalization metrics. The authors decompose memory into three orthogonal dimensions: behavioral consistency (maintaining user style and voice), factual presence (recalling facts from user history), and factual absence (correctly abstaining when facts are absent). By comparing gamma-LoRA (per-user fine-tuned adapters) against retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) using BGE-large dense retrieval on synthetic and real datasets, they demonstrate that gamma-LoRA excels at behavioral style while RAG excels at factual absence. Crucially, they identify specific query-projection cells in attention layers 21-35 that causally load-bear both effects in opposite directions: zeroing these LoRA weights increases absence-probe true positive rate by 33 percentage points while dropping presence-probe performance by 20 percentage points. The asymmetry persists and even strengthens on the more heavily RLHF-tuned Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model.
connects: This work extends prior research on personalization in language models and retrieval-augmented generation by moving beyond monolithic evaluation metrics toward mechanistic understanding of failure modes. It builds on parametric versus non-parametric memory trade-offs documented in previous retrieval studies and connects to mechanistic interpretability work identifying how transformers route information through specific attention heads and weight matrices. The paper responds to limitations in existing personalization benchmarks like LaMP that aggregate performance across distinct capabilities, and it builds on adapter-based fine-tuning research including LoRA by introducing per-user variants and analyzing their causal effects on model behavior. The work also engages with recent findings on RLHF's effects on model representations and robustness.
enables: This diagnostic framework opens multiple research directions for improving user-side memory in LLMs. The identification of specific causally-relevant cells suggests targeted intervention strategies that could resolve the asymmetry by selectively modifying or gating different pathways for different memory functions. The findings motivate hybrid approaches that leverage gamma-LoRA's strengths in behavioral consistency while incorporating RAG's superior factual absence handling, potentially through learned routing mechanisms or task-specific pathway selection. The mechanistic insights enable future work investigating whether similar factorization patterns appear in other personalization dimensions or across different model architectures. The framework also supports development of more granular personalization metrics and evaluation benchmarks that decompose aggregate performance to catch opposite-direction failures in deployed systems.
